claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 4 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to understand your situation.

**Do you have a child (or children) you're claiming for?** 

If yes, please tell me:
- How many children you want to claim for
- The age of each child (or if they're under 16, between 16-19, or 20 or older)

This will help me guide you through the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, I have two children I'd like to claim for. 

I have Alex, who's 6 years old, and Blake, who's 4. They're both quite young, so still in primary school age.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me assess the eligibility for both Alex (age 6) and Blake (age 4).

Since both children are under 16, they potentially qualify as "qualifying children" for Child Benefit purposes. Let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, the next question:

**Are Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since they're both 6 and 4 years old and in primary school age, I expect the answer is that they're not working at all, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh no, they're not working at all! They're just little ones – Alex is only 6 and Blake is 4. They're far too young for any kind of job. Alex is in primary school and Blake is still in reception.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, that makes sense! Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

In other words, are they receiving any government benefits directly in their own name? Given their young ages (6 and 4), this would be very unusual, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receives any benefits in their own name. They're just young children – they don't get Universal Credit or ESA or anything like that. Nothing like that at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT in any apprenticeship programs?**

This is another formality given their ages (6 and 4), but I need to confirm that neither child is enrolled in any type of apprenticeship scheme.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not! They're far too young for apprenticeships. Alex is only 6 and Blake is 4 – they're just in primary school. No apprenticeships or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?** You can qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**, or
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold), or
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work, or
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources, or
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources, or
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national, or
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm based in Manchester, so I definitely meet the residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check your responsibility for the children.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?** You can qualify if ANY of the following apply:

1. **You live with the children**, or
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care - things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents), or
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you for both Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Well, it's a bit different for each of them actually. Alex lives at home with me, so I'm definitely responsible for him. 

But Blake doesn't live with me - he lives with my ex-partner. I do help out a bit with some money for clothes and things, but it's not a huge amount - just £20 a week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining the situation. This is important information as the responsibility requirements differ between Alex and Blake.

Let me check the Child Benefit rates to see if your contribution for Blake meets the minimum threshold:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I can see that the specific payment rates aren't listed in my reference data. Let me explain the situation:

**For Alex (age 6):** You clearly meet the responsibility requirement since Alex lives with you at home.

**For Blake (age 4):** You're contributing £20 per week towards Blake's upkeep. To qualify as "responsible" for a child you don't live with, you need to contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit payment amount.

The current Child Benefit rates (as of 2024) are:
- Eldest/only child: £25.60 per week
- Additional children: £16.95 per week

Since Blake would be your second child (if claiming for both), you'd need to contribute at least £16.95 per week to meet the responsibility requirement. **Your £20 per week contribution exceeds this amount**, so you do meet the responsibility requirement for Blake.

However, there's an important question: **Is your ex-partner currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?** Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time. If your ex-partner is already claiming for Blake, that could affect your eligibility to claim.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I see. No, my ex-partner isn't claiming Child Benefit for Blake. So that shouldn't be a problem then.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since your ex-partner isn't claiming Child Benefit for Blake, we can proceed. Let me continue with the next check:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check the living arrangements for both children.

**For Alex and Blake, are either of them:**
- Currently in hospital (as an in-patient)?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally (Alex with you, Blake with your ex-partner)?

Please let me know the situation for both children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're both living normally - Alex lives at home with me, and Blake lives with my ex-partner. Neither of them is in hospital, care, or being fostered. They're just in regular family situations.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex or Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the council paying for either child's living costs or upkeep? (This would generally mean they're not eligible if the council is paying.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards either of them. Alex lives with me and I support him, and Blake lives with my ex-partner. No council involvement for either child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Excellent News! You Are Eligible for Child Benefit! 🎉

Based on my assessment, **you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake**.

### Summary for Each Child:

**Alex (age 6):**
✅ **ELIGIBLE** - You meet all the requirements as Alex lives with you at home and you're responsible for his care.

**Blake (age 4):**
✅ **ELIGIBLE** - Even though Blake lives with your ex-partner, you meet the responsibility requirement by contributing £20 per week towards his upkeep, which exceeds the minimum Child Benefit rate of £16.95 per week for additional children. Importantly, your ex-partner is not currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

### Your Child Benefit Payment:
You'll receive:
- **£25.60 per week** for Alex (as the eldest child)
- **£16.95 per week** for Blake (as an additional child)
- **Total: £42.55 per week** (£184.05 per month)

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** for Alex and Blake
4. **Payments will be made every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Important Note - High Income Child Benefit Charge:
If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge through your tax return. This doesn't affect your eligibility, but it's something to be aware of.

You can apply at: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Do you have any questions about the claim process?

reasonings: Alex (age 6) is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old, making him a qualifying child; (2) He is not in paid work; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by living in Manchester; (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together; (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living normally at home with the claimant; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 4) is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old, making him a qualifying child; (2) He is not in paid work; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by living in Manchester; (6) The claimant is responsible for Blake by contributing £20 per week towards his upkeep (clothes and other expenses), which exceeds the minimum Child Benefit rate of £16.95 per week for additional children; (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake (the ex-partner is not claiming); (8) Blake is living normally with the claimant's ex-partner; (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for both children. Alex (6) lives with the claimant, meeting the responsibility requirement through residence. Blake (4) lives with the claimant's ex-partner, but the claimant meets the responsibility requirement by contributing £20 per week towards Blake's upkeep, which exceeds the £16.95 weekly Child Benefit rate for additional children. All other eligibility criteria are satisfied for both children, and importantly, no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for either child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 6) is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old, making him a qualifying child; (2) He is not in paid work; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by living in Manchester; (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together; (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living normally at home with the claimant; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 4) is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old, making him a qualifying child; (2) He is not in paid work; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by living in Manchester; (6) The claimant is responsible for Blake by contributing £20 per week towards his upkeep (clothes and other expenses), which exceeds the minimum Child Benefit rate of £16.95 per week for additional children; (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake (the ex-partner is not claiming); (8) Blake is living normally with the claimant's ex-partner; (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for both children. Alex (6) lives with the claimant, meeting the responsibility requirement through residence. Blake (4) lives with the claimant's ex-partner, but the claimant meets the responsibility requirement by contributing £20 per week towards Blake's upkeep, which exceeds the £16.95 weekly Child Benefit rate for additional children. All other eligibility criteria are satisfied for both children, and importantly, no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for either child.
